content.text: Long-term memory is any storage that persists information across agent runs. Unlike short-term memory (the
messages

list, which resets when the run ends), long-term memory survives process restarts and is accessible to future runs of the same agent. The model cannot read long-term memory directly — it can only access it through tool calls that query the store and inject results into the
messages

list. Four storage types cover the main use cases: key-value stores for fast exact lookups, document stores for structured run records, vector stores for semantic retrieval, and relational databases for analytics and cross-run queries.

attrs.alt: Long-term memory — four storage types and when to use each

content.text: Key-Value Memory

The simplest long-term memory is a key-value store — a persistent dictionary keyed by string identifiers. It is the right choice for agent state, run metadata, and deduplication sets, where exact lookup by a known key is all that is needed:
SQLite is a practical default

— it requires no external service, persists to a file, and supports concurrent reads. For multi-process agents or distributed systems, Redis or DynamoDB provide the same interface with better concurrency.
JSON-serialise all values
— storing values as JSON strings makes the store language-agnostic and lets you inspect the database directly.
json.dumps
json.loads
adds negligible overhead.
Key naming matters
— use a consistent scheme (
"run:<id>:status"
"agent:<name>:last_run"
) so keys from different agents or runs do not collide.
Document Store

When each run produces a structured artifact — a findings record, an audit log, a plan — a document store provides a natural home. Each document has a unique ID (often the run ID) and can be loaded, updated, or queried by future runs:

import json
from pathlib import Path
from dataclasses import dataclass, asdict


@dataclass
class RunRecord:
    """Persistent record of one agent run, stored as a JSON document."""
    run_id: str
    task: str
    status: str                    # "running", "done", "failed"
    files_modified: list[str]
    findings: list[str]
    duration_seconds: float = 0.0


class DocumentStore:
    """File-backed document store. One JSON file per run."""

    def __init__(self, base_dir: str = "runs/"):
        self.base = Path(base_dir)
        self.base.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

    def save(self, doc_id: str, data: object) -> Path:
        path = self.base / f"{doc_id}.json"
        with open(path, "w") as f:
            json.dump(data if isinstance(data, dict) else asdict(data), f, indent=2)
        return path

    def load(self, doc_id: str) -> dict | None:
        path = self.base / f"{doc_id}.json"
        if not path.exists():
            return None
        with open(path) as f:
            return json.load(f)

    def list_ids(self) -> list[str]:
        return [p.stem for p in self.base.glob("*.json")]


store = DocumentStore()

# At the start of a run:
record = RunRecord(
    run_id="run-42",
    task="Fix login bug",
    status="running",
    files_modified=[],
    findings=[],
)
store.save(record.run_id, record)

# At the end of a run, update:
record.status = "done"
record.files_modified = ["src/auth.py"]
record.findings = ["password not URL-decoded at auth.py:42"]
store.save(record.run_id, record)
One document per run is the standard pattern
— using the run ID as the document ID makes it easy to load any run's record for debugging or continuation.
Update in place rather than append
— keeping a single record per run (with status fields) is simpler than maintaining a sequence of deltas. Load, modify, and save.
File-backed stores work for small deployments
— a directory of JSON files is readable by humans, requires no infrastructure, and is sufficient for hundreds of runs. Use a database-backed store (MongoDB, Firestore) when the volume grows.
Vector Store (Semantic Memory)

A vector store allows fuzzy retrieval: finding stored documents that are semantically similar to a query, even when exact keywords do not match. This is useful for retrieving relevant past experience, documentation, or accumulated knowledge before a run:

# Vector stores enable semantic (fuzzy) retrieval: find documents similar to a query
# even when exact keywords don't match.
#
# Pattern: embed → store → query → inject top-k results into messages.

# Using chromadb (pip install chromadb) as an example:
import chromadb
import anthropic

# Build an embedding with the Anthropic API (text-embedding-3-small from OpenAI also works)
def embed(text: str) -> list[float]:
    """Stub: replace with your preferred embedding model."""
    # Example with a HuggingFace sentence transformer or an embedding API
    raise NotImplementedError("Plug in your embedding model here")


class SemanticMemory:
    """Semantic memory backed by a Chroma vector store."""

    def __init__(self, collection_name: str = "agent_memory"):
        self.client = chromadb.Client()
        self.col = self.client.get_or_create_collection(collection_name)

    def add(self, doc_id: str, text: str, metadata: dict | None = None) -> None:
        """Store a document with its embedding."""
        self.col.add(
            ids=[doc_id],
            documents=[text],
            metadatas=[metadata or {}],
        )

    def query(self, text: str, top_k: int = 5) -> list[dict]:
        """Return the top_k most similar documents."""
        results = self.col.query(query_texts=[text], n_results=top_k)
        return [
            {"id": id_, "text": doc, "metadata": meta}
            for id_, doc, meta in zip(
                results["ids"][0],
                results["documents"][0],
                results["metadatas"][0],
            )
        ]


# Usage: inject retrieved context into the task prompt
sem = SemanticMemory()
sem.add("run-41-summary", "Fixed login bug: password was not URL-decoded in auth.py")
sem.add("run-38-summary", "Refactored session handling to use a context manager")

# Before the agent run, retrieve relevant past experience
relevant = sem.query("login authentication session", top_k=3)
context = "\n".join(f"- {r['text']}" for r in relevant)
task = f"Fix the new auth bug.\n\nRelevant past experience:\n{context}"
Inject retrieved context into messages[0]
— the model cannot query the vector store directly. Retrieve relevant documents before the run starts and include them in the initial user turn as "relevant past experience".
Use for retrieval, not for state

— vector stores are optimised for similarity search, not for storing structured agent state. Use a key-value or document store for run records and status; use a vector store for unstructured knowledge.
top_k matters for context budget
— retrieving 10 documents at 500 tokens each adds 5,000 tokens to the context. Choose
top_k
based on the relevance of your use case and the available context budget.
Long-Term Memory in the Agent Loop
The standard pattern is: load from long-term memory before the run, use short-term memory (messages list) during the run, and write to long-term memory at the end:
Read at start, write at end
— reading long-term memory before
messages[0]
is constructed injects prior context without growing the messages list. Writing at the end (when
stop_reason == "end_turn"
) captures the outcome before the run is discarded.
Separate the two memory types
— long-term memory is for persistence across runs; short-term memory is for reasoning within a run. Mixing them (e.g., saving every message to a database) wastes storage and obscures the distinction.
Extract structured data, not raw messages
_extract_modified_files
shows the pattern: mine the
messages
list for structured facts before the run ends. Raw message lists are large and hard to query.
Deduplication Across Runs

A common use for long-term memory is deduplication: preventing a multi-run agent from processing the same item twice. A persistent set of processed IDs, backed by the key-value store, enables this cleanly:
Persist the set, not just the count
— storing the full set of processed IDs makes it possible to resume after a partial run. A count alone cannot tell you which items were already done.
Check before processing, mark after
— checking
is_done
before starting an item is cheaper than discovering a duplicate mid-way through. Mark
mark_done
only after the item is fully processed.
Use item-level IDs, not run IDs
— deduplication is per item (issue number, file path, record ID), not per run. Run IDs are for the document store; item IDs are for the processed set.
Testing Memory Components
Summary

Long-term memory persists information across agent runs through external storage. The four types are: key-value (exact lookup, agent state), document (structured run records), vector (semantic retrieval, past experience), and relational (analytics, cross-run queries). The model accesses long-term memory only through tool calls — it cannot query a store directly. The standard loop pattern is: load relevant long-term memory before constructing
messages[0]

, use the messages list for in-run reasoning, and write a structured outcome record to long-term memory when the run ends. Use persistent sets for cross-run deduplication. Extract structured facts from the messages list before persisting — avoid storing raw message lists.
